description:
You are acting as a junior security auditor who has been asked to verify that at-least one self-auditing cron job is configured correctly for the current (non-root) user.  
Your deliverable is a small audit workspace under “/home/user/audit_cron” that must contain:

1. Directory  
   • /home/user/audit_cron — must exist and be owned by the current user, permissions 700.  

2. Script file  
   • /home/user/audit_cron/cron_security_audit.sh (owner: current user, mode: 700).  
   • Mandatory first line: the standard bash she-bang (“#!/bin/bash”).  
   • When the script is executed it must append exactly **one** single-line report in the format shown below to /home/user/audit_cron/cron_audit.log.  
     REPORT-LINE FORMAT (exact, no extra spaces):  
        YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS | CRONTAB_LINES:<N>  
     ‑ The timestamp must come from the local system clock (output of the “date '+%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S'” command).  
     ‑ <N> is the count of non-comment, non-blank lines in the user’s current crontab at execution time.  

   Example of a valid report line (spacing and bar character are significant):  
        2034-01-02 08:00:17 | CRONTAB_LINES:3  

3. Log file  
   • /home/user/audit_cron/cron_audit.log — must exist after you finish.  
   • After you run the script manually once, the file must contain at least one correctly formatted report line (as defined above) followed by a newline character.  
   • No other content is allowed in the file except additional, identically formatted report lines that cron will append in the future.

4. Cron job  
   • The current user’s crontab must contain exactly one new entry that executes the script every 30 minutes.  
   • The line must use absolute paths and must redirect both STDOUT and STDERR to the log file that you just created.  
   • The resulting crontab line must be **identical** (spacing included) to:  
        */30 * * * * /home/user/audit_cron/cron_security_audit.sh >> /home/user/audit_cron/cron_audit.log 2>&1  

5. One-time setup report  
   • /home/user/audit_cron/setup_report.json — a UTF-8 JSON document with exactly four top-level keys (order does not matter):  
        {
          "script_path": "/home/user/audit_cron/cron_security_audit.sh",
          "script_permission": "700",
          "cron_schedule_line": "*/30 * * * * /home/user/audit_cron/cron_security_audit.sh >> /home/user/audit_cron/cron_audit.log 2>&1",
          "log_file_exists": true
        }

Checklist of what the automated grader will verify:

A. Directory, script, log and JSON files exist at the exact paths given.  
B. Permissions: directory (700), script (700) and log file not world writable.  
C. First line of script is "#!/bin/bash".  
D. Crontab of the current user contains only one line that exactly matches the required schedule line (comments and other existing lines are acceptable, but there must be one—and only one—match).  
E. You manually executed the script once so cron_audit.log already contains ≥1 line matching the regex:  
        ^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2} [0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2} \| CRONTAB_LINES:[0-9]+$  
F. setup_report.json parses as valid JSON and holds the four required keys with the exact values specified above.

Produce no extraneous output in any of those files; all your diagnostic output can go to the terminal.  When everything matches these conditions, the grading script will consider the task complete.
truth:
Pre-existing state (before the agent starts):  
• The user’s home is /home/user.  
• /home/user/audit_cron does NOT exist.  
• The default crontab for the user is empty.  

Expected end state for grading:

1. /home/user/audit_cron:  
   - exists, owner: user, group: user, mode: 0700.

2. /home/user/audit_cron/cron_security_audit.sh:  
   - Content (exact byte-for-byte except for whitespace inside commands):  
        #!/bin/bash
        ts=$(date '+%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S')
        lines=$(crontab -l 2>/dev/null | grep -v '^[[:space:]]*#' | grep -v '^[[:space:]]*$' | wc -l)
        echo "$ts | CRONTAB_LINES:$lines"
   - Note: the last echo line must *NOT* redirect; redirection is done by cron.  
   - Owner user, mode 0700.

3. /home/user/audit_cron/cron_audit.log:  
   - Plain-text.  
   - First line matches regex described in the task.  
   - At least one newline-terminated line.  
   - No other content at grading time (only 1 line expected).

4. User’s crontab (output of “crontab -l”):  
   - Contains exactly one line that matches:  
        */30 * * * * /home/user/audit_cron/cron_security_audit.sh >> /home/user/audit_cron/cron_audit.log 2>&1

5. /home/user/audit_cron/setup_report.json:  
   - Exactly this JSON (no pretty-printing required, but values exactly):  
        {"script_path":"/home/user/audit_cron/cron_security_audit.sh","script_permission":"700","cron_schedule_line":"*/30 * * * * /home/user/audit_cron/cron_security_audit.sh >> /home/user/audit_cron/cron_audit.log 2>&1","log_file_exists":true}

6. Permissions of cron_audit.log: group-writable is OK; world-writable is NOT OK.

Grading script actions:  
• Verify paths & permissions.  
• Use grep/regex on crontab.  
• Parse log file & JSON.  
• Execute the script once more to make sure it appends correctly (second line pattern).

The agent has no sudo access; therefore all paths must be inside /home/user.
